perturbation.original_text: At any time upon request from the Disclosing Party or upon the conclusion of the Purpose or expiry of this Agreement, the Receiving Party, at its own cost, will return or procure the return, promptly and in any event within 14 days of receipt of such request, of each and every copy of Confidential Information given by the Disclosing Party, and satisfy the Disclosing Party that it no longer holds any further Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time upon request from the Disclosing Party or upon the conclusion of the Purpose or expiry of this Agreement, the Receiving Party, at its own cost, will return or procure the return within 60 days of receipt of such request, of each and every copy of Confidential Information given by the Disclosing Party, and satisfy the Disclosing Party that it no longer holds any further Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return period to 60 days may not be considered 'prompt' enough under general contract law principles, especially if the information is time-sensitive. While there isn't a specific statute dictating the exact timeframe, a longer, undefined period creates ambiguity and weakens the enforceability compared to a reasonable, shorter duration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract and Reasonable Time
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 47 of the Indian Contract Act deals with the time for performance of a contract, stating that when a promisor has undertaken to do a thing, and the time has not been specified, the promise must be performed within a reasonable time. Extending the return period to 60 days might be deemed unreasonable in many contexts, potentially conflicting with the implicit requirement for promptness and hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information effectively. This ambiguity could constitute a breach if 'prompt' is interpreted as significantly shorter.
perturbation.location: V. Copying and Return of Furnished Instruments: 3
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction. The original text stipulates a 'prompt' return of confidential information within 14 days, while the changed text extends this to 60 days. The Law Explanation states that Section 47 of the Indian Contract Act requires performance within a reasonable time when no time is specified. The change from 'prompt' and '14 days' to '60 days' could be deemed unreasonable, conflicting with the implied requirement for promptness. Although the scraped snippets are missing, the Law Explanation indicates a potential breach if 'prompt' is interpreted as significantly shorter, and the Perturbation Explanation supports this. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition is met, where time-sensitive obligations are altered, and the 60-day deadline could violate the implicit 'reasonable time' requirement under Section 47.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in force for five (5) years from the Effective Date, except to the extent this Agreement is superseded by stipulations of the Contemplated Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in force for ten (10) years from the Effective Date, except to the extent this Agreement is superseded by stipulations of the Contemplated Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the agreement to 10 years could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade under Indian law if the scope of confidential information is overly broad or the restrictions on the Receiving Party are excessive.  While NDAs can last for several years, 10 years requires careful consideration of the specific circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_citation: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act states that every agreement by which anyone is restrained from exercising a lawful profession, trade or business of any kind, is to that extent void.  A ten-year NDA, depending on the nature of the confidential information and the scope of restrictions, could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade if it unduly restricts the Receiving Party's ability to conduct business or practice their profession. The reasonableness is highly contextual, but extending the term increases the risk.
perturbation.justification: The change extends the agreement from 5 to 10 years. The perturbation and law explanations suggest that a 10-year NDA could be an unreasonable restraint of trade under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, particularly if the confidential information's scope or restrictions are excessive. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the extended duration could violate federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The rights and obligations of each Party with respect to all Confidential Information of the other Party that is received under this Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The rights and obligations of each Party with respect to all Confidential Information of the other Party that is received under this Agreement shall remain in effect indefinitely from the date of disclosure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Making the confidentiality obligations indefinite may be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade, as such a clause might be deemed overly broad and prevent the Receiving Party from freely conducting business. Indian contract law requires reasonable restrictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_citation: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act prohibits agreements that restrain anyone from exercising a lawful profession, trade, or business. An indefinite restriction on the use of confidential information, particularly if the definition of confidential information is broad, could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade and therefore unenforceable. Courts often look at the scope and duration of the restriction when determining reasonableness.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a five-year limit on confidentiality obligations, while the changed text makes them indefinite. The perturbation and law explanations indicate that indefinite restrictions on the use of confidential information can be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, potentially making the clause unenforceable. Since the changed contract creates a clause that could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, it contradicts the principles outlined in the Indian Contract Act.

perturbation.original_text: Upon 45 days written notice, the Disclosing Party may audit the use of the programs, materials, marketing materials, services, and such additional disclosed resources. The Receiving Party agrees to co-operate with the Disclosing Party’s audit and to provide reasonable assistance and access to information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon 2 days written notice, the Disclosing Party may audit the use of the programs, materials, marketing materials, services, and such additional disclosed resources. The Receiving Party agrees to co-operate with the Disclosing Party’s audit and to provide reasonable assistance and access to information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period to 2 days for audits may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient under general contract law principles, potentially hindering the Receiving Party's ability to adequately prepare for the audit and comply with its obligations. This could be seen as a breach of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract and Reasonable Time
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 47 of the Indian Contract Act requires promises to be performed within a reasonable time if no time is specified. While the contract does specify a time frame, the two-day notice is arguably unreasonably short, preventing the Receiving Party from reasonably preparing for the audit. A court may interpret this as a breach of the implied duty to act in good faith, or deem the timeframe unreasonable and therefore unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a 45-day notice period for audits, while the changed text reduces this to 2 days. The Law Explanation argues that a 2-day notice is 'unreasonably short' and potentially a breach of the duty of good faith under Section 47 of the Indian Contract Act, which requires promises to be performed within a reasonable time if no time is specified. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that this timeframe may be deemed unreasonable under general contract law, hindering the Receiving Party's ability to prepare. Although no specific law snippets were provided, the consensus between the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation is that the 2-day notice period creates a legal contradiction by being potentially 'unreasonable' and undermining the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a breach or threatened breach by the Receiving Party of any provisions of this Agreement, the Disclosing Party, in addition to and not in limitation of any other rights, remedies or damages available to the Disclosing Party at law or in equity, shall be entitled to a temporary restraining order / preliminary injunction in order to prevent or to restrain any such breach by the Receiving Party, or by any or all persons directly or indirectly acting for, on behalf of, or with the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a breach or threatened breach by the Receiving Party of any provisions of this Agreement, the Disclosing Party, shall be entitled to an immediate seizure of all assets of the Receiving Party without any prior notice or judicial intervention.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing immediate seizure of assets without notice or judicial intervention is a violation of due process and legal principles related to property rights.  This is an overly broad and punitive measure that bypasses the Indian legal system.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 21 of the Constitution of India
perturbation.law_citation: Article 21 of the Constitution of India
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 21 of the Constitution of India guarantees the right to life and personal liberty, which has been interpreted by the Supreme Court to include the right to property.  Depriving someone of their property without due process of law – i.e., without notice, a hearing, or judicial oversight – is a direct violation of Article 21. The changed text allows for arbitrary seizure of assets, bypassing the legal system and depriving the Receiving Party of their fundamental rights.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clear contradiction by allowing immediate seizure of assets without any prior notice or judicial intervention. This directly violates fundamental legal principles related to due process and property rights, as highlighted in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. The original text allows for a temporary restraining order/preliminary injunction. The changed text now allows asset seizure with no due process whatsoever.
